segments.title: HMS Foxglove's long Acacia-class career
segments.summary: Meet HMS Foxglove, an Acacia-class Royal Navy minesweeper launched in 1915 whose service extended to 1946.
segments.body: Foxglove is introduced as an Acacia-class minesweeper built at Barclay Curle in Glasgow and commissioned in 1915. Her eventual scrapping in 1946 frames the video as the career of a small auxiliary vessel that remained useful across several decades. The opening also establishes minesweeping as the class's original purpose.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Foxglove as an Acacia-class Royal Navy minesweeper, gives her 1915 launch and commission, and notes her 1946 scrapping.

segments.title: A compact minesweeper with adaptable armament
segments.summary: Foxglove's dimensions and guns show how a modest minesweeper could receive different armament fits during service.
segments.body: The ship displaced about 1,200 tons and measured roughly 250 to 262 feet long, with a narrow beam and shallow draft. Her intended armament was two 12-pounders and two 3-pounders, but the account stresses that ships did not always retain their nominal fit. Foxglove appears at times to have carried four-inch guns and additional 3-pounders, a useful reminder that wartime and service requirements could alter a small warship's weapons.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Foxglove's displacement and dimensions, then contrasts her designed 12-pounder and 3-pounder armament with reported four-inch-gun fits.

segments.title: From World War I minesweeping to wider wartime service
segments.summary: Foxglove spent her first two wartime years sweeping mines before moving into broader fighting duties in 1917.
segments.body: Foxglove's early World War I work was primarily minesweeping, the practical and dangerous task for which the Acacia class had been built. In 1917 she moved away from that narrow role into wider wartime employment. The shift illustrates how a vessel designed for one specialist duty could be drawn into more general naval service as wartime demands evolved.

evidence.note: The transcript says Foxglove performed minesweeping duties until 1917 before being turned to broader war-fighting work.

segments.title: China Station rescue work with HMS Carlisle
segments.summary: On the China Station, Foxglove helped HMS Carlisle rescue survivors from the grounded SS Hong Moh in 1921.
segments.body: During her 1920s China Station service, Foxglove was involved in assisting the grounded SS Hong Moh near White Rock off Lamock Island. Working with the C-class cruiser HMS Carlisle, she helped rescue 221 survivors in total. The episode shows the broad peacetime utility of a small Royal Navy vessel far from home waters, where rescue and assistance could be as important as combat readiness.

evidence.note: The transcript places Foxglove on the China Station and describes the 3 March 1921 rescue effort with HMS Carlisle, including 221 survivors.

segments.title: Damage, conversion, and service to 1946
segments.summary: German dive bombers damaged Foxglove off the Isle of Wight, but she remained afloat and continued the war in support roles.
segments.body: Foxglove's active World War II contribution was cut short when German aircraft dive-bombed and damaged her off the Isle of Wight. Because she stayed afloat, the Royal Navy converted her first into an accommodation and base ship, then into a harbour guard ship at Londonderry for the remainder of the war. Her subsequent Mediterranean work and the final assessment of her long service underline the value drawn from a durable small ship before her 1946 scrapping.

evidence.note: The transcript describes dive-bomb damage off the Isle of Wight, conversion to accommodation and base-ship roles, harbour guard service at Londonderry, and 1946 scrapping.
The closing remarks mention China Station and Mediterranean service, note that active World War II work ended early in July 1940, and judge the ship a valuable build.
